criteria: SELECT parser produces an AstNode of type NODE_SELECT with a column array containing NODE_WILDCARD for * and expression nodes for named columns
SELECT parser extracts the FROM clause table name as a heap-allocated null-terminated string
SELECT parser produces an optional WHERE subtree as an expression AstNode; where_expr is NULL when no WHERE clause is present
SELECT parser produces an optional ORDER BY array of OrderByItem structs, each with an expression node and an ascending flag (1=ASC, 0=DESC)
SELECT parser produces an optional LIMIT clause as an expression AstNode; limit_expr is NULL when no LIMIT clause is present
INSERT parser produces an AstNode of type NODE_INSERT with the target table name
INSERT parser populates column_names array when an explicit column list is provided before VALUES; column_names is NULL otherwise
INSERT parser produces a values array containing one expression AstNode per value in the VALUES (...) list
CREATE TABLE parser produces an AstNode of type NODE_CREATE_TABLE with the table name and an array of ColumnDef structs
Each ColumnDef struct contains name (heap-allocated string), type_name (heap-allocated string), is_primary_key, is_not_null, and is_unique integer flags
CREATE TABLE parser correctly sets is_primary_key=1 for columns with PRIMARY KEY constraint
CREATE TABLE parser correctly sets is_not_null=1 for columns with NOT NULL constraint
CREATE TABLE parser correctly sets is_unique=1 for columns with UNIQUE constraint
Expression parser assigns left binding powers: OR=10, AND=20, comparison operators (=,!=,<,>,<=,>=,IS)=30, addition/subtraction=40, multiplication/division=50
Expression parser correctly parses 'a AND b OR c' as OR(AND(a,b), c) — AND binds tighter than OR
Expression parser correctly parses 'a OR b AND c' as OR(a, AND(b,c)) — AND binds tighter than OR
NOT unary operator produces NODE_UNARY_EXPR with op=TOKEN_NOT and binds to the immediately following comparison, not the entire AND/OR chain
Parenthesized subexpressions correctly override default precedence: '(a OR b) AND c' produces AND(OR(a,b), c)
Binary expressions (AND, OR, all comparison operators) produce NODE_BINARY_EXPR nodes with correct op, left, and right fields
Unary NOT expression produces NODE_UNARY_EXPR node with op=TOKEN_NOT and a single operand field
NULL keyword is parsed as NODE_LITERAL_NULL, not NODE_IDENTIFIER
Integer literals produce NODE_LITERAL_INT nodes with the correct int64_t value parsed from the token span
Float literals produce NODE_LITERAL_FLOAT nodes with the correct double value parsed from the token span
String literals produce NODE_LITERAL_STRING nodes with decoded value (outer quotes stripped, '' sequences replaced with single ')
Column references produce NODE_IDENTIFIER nodes with the identifier name; table.column syntax produces NODE_COLUMN_REF with both table and name fields populated
Parser reports error position (line and column) for every syntax error via error_line and error_col fields in ParseResult
Parser reports a meaningful human-readable error_msg string identifying what was expected and what was found for every syntax error
Tokenizer errors (TOKEN_ERROR in token stream) are detected before parsing begins and surfaced as ParseResult errors with position
ast_free correctly releases all heap-allocated memory in an AstNode tree including strings and child node arrays
Test suite passes for 15+ valid SQL statements spanning SELECT (with and without WHERE/ORDER BY/LIMIT), INSERT (with and without column list), and CREATE TABLE (with multiple constraints)
Test suite correctly rejects 10+ invalid SQL statements, each producing had_error=1 with non-empty error_msg and valid error_line/error_col
Parser integrates with lexer_tokenize_all output — token stream from Milestone 1 lexer is correctly consumed without modification
Parser handles IS NOT NULL as a binary expression on the identifier (equivalent to inequality with NULL)
Type names INTEGER, TEXT, REAL, BLOB in CREATE TABLE are accepted as TOKEN_IDENTIFIER tokens and stored verbatim in ColumnDef.type_name

Compiler translates SELECT AST into a bytecode program with at minimum the following opcodes: OP_OPEN_TABLE (cursor open), OP_REWIND (seek to first row with empty-table jump), OP_COLUMN (load column into register), OP_RESULT_ROW (emit result), OP_NEXT (advance cursor with loop-back jump), OP_HALT (terminate program)

Compiler translates INSERT AST into bytecode with at minimum: OP_OPEN_TABLE (write mode), value-loading opcodes for each VALUES expression, OP_MAKE_RECORD (serialize registers to binary record), OP_INSERT (write to B-tree via cursor), OP_HALT
Virtual machine executes bytecode programs in a fetch-decode-execute loop: each iteration reads prog->instructions[pc], switches on opcode, executes the operation, and increments or jumps pc

VM maintains a statically-allocated register file (array of Value structs) indexed by register number; each Value has a type tag (VAL_NULL, VAL_INTEGER, VAL_REAL, VAL_TEXT, VAL_BLOB) and a union of typed data
EXPLAIN command invoked on any valid SQL statement produces a human-readable table listing address, opcode name, p1, p2, p3 fields, and a comment for each instruction in the compiled program

WHERE clause with a single comparison operator (=, !=, <, >, <=, >=) compiles to: OP_COLUMN loading the column, a value-loading opcode for the literal, and an inverted comparison opcode (e.g., WHERE age > 18 → OP_LE that jumps past OP_RESULT_ROW to OP_NEXT when age <= 18)
WHERE clause with AND operator compiles so that if either sub-condition fails, the row is skipped to OP_NEXT without evaluating remaining conditions
WHERE clause with OR operator compiles so that if the left sub-condition succeeds, the right sub-condition is skipped

All conditional jump instructions (OP_REWIND, OP_NEXT, OP_EQ, OP_NE, OP_LT, OP_LE, OP_GT, OP_GE, OP_IF, OP_IF_NOT) are emitted with correct target addresses via forward-reference patching: emit placeholder, emit body, call patch_jump to fill in target
OP_NEXT p2 field points to an address before the current OP_NEXT (back to loop body start), not forward; empty table causes OP_REWIND to jump past the loop to OP_CLOSE/OP_HALT
Every compiled program ends with OP_HALT as the final instruction; the VM's fetch loop must not advance past OP_HALT
value_compare returns INT32_MAX when either operand is VAL_NULL; comparison opcodes treat INT32_MAX as non-matching (skip condition not taken for NULL) — WHERE x = NULL never evaluates to true
Register allocation uses monotonically increasing next_reg counter; no two distinct live values occupy the same register index in the same compiled program
Cursor abstraction: OP_COLUMN, OP_REWIND, OP_NEXT, OP_INSERT delegate to btree_cursor_* functions; the VM has no direct knowledge of page layout or B-tree structure
VM executes a full table scan of 10,000 rows (all pages in buffer pool / stub cursor) via SELECT * FROM t in under 100ms on a standard development machine

SELECT with named columns (not *) compiles each column reference to a separate OP_COLUMN instruction loading the correct column index into a distinct register, followed by a single OP_RESULT_ROW spanning all allocated registers
SELECT * compiles to one OP_COLUMN per column in the table schema (retrieved via schema_get_column_count), followed by OP_RESULT_ROW with count equal to the schema column count
Integer literals in the range INT32_MIN..INT32_MAX compile to OP_INTEGER with the value in p1; larger integers compile to OP_INT64 with the value in p4.i64
String literal values in compiled programs point to heap-allocated decoded strings (outer quotes stripped, '' unescaped to ') produced during AST parsing, not into raw SQL source text
OP_NULL stores VAL_NULL in the destination register; OP_INSERT with VAL_NULL in the rowid register calls btree_next_rowid to auto-assign the next available rowid
BufferPool struct is allocated with a configurable number of page frames (default 1000) and each frame is exactly 4096 bytes
buffer_pool_create() accepts a file descriptor and frame count, allocates all frame memory upfront, and returns a valid BufferPool handle
buffer_pool_fetch() returns a non-NULL pointer to the page's 4096-byte data buffer when the page is already in the pool (cache hit)
buffer_pool_fetch() reads the page from disk using pread() when the page is not in the pool (cache miss), installs it in a frame, and returns a pinned pointer
Each call to buffer_pool_fetch() increments the frame's pin_count, preventing eviction until buffer_pool_unpin() is called
buffer_pool_unpin() decrements the frame's pin_count; passing dirty=1 sets the frame's is_dirty flag
LRU eviction correctly selects the frame at the tail of the LRU list (least recently used) when all frames are occupied and a new page must be loaded
LRU eviction skips frames with pin_count > 0; only unpinned frames are eligible for eviction
Evicting a dirty frame writes its 4096-byte contents to the correct file offset (page_id * 4096) using pwrite() before the frame is reused
buffer_pool_fetch() moves the accessed frame to the head of the LRU list on every access (hit or miss)
buffer_pool_fetch() returns NULL when all frames are pinned (pool exhausted), without corrupting any existing frame
buffer_pool_new_page() allocates the next sequential page_id, zeroes the frame contents with memset(), marks the frame dirty, pins it, and returns a valid pointer
buffer_pool_flush_page() writes a specific dirty page to disk using pwrite() and clears its is_dirty flag
buffer_pool_flush_all() iterates all frames, writes every dirty frame to disk using pwrite(), and returns the count of pages flushed
buffer_pool_flush_all() does NOT call fsync() internally; fsync responsibility belongs to the caller
Page IDs form a linear address space where page_id N maps to file offset N * 4096
The page table (hash map) maps PageId to frame index in O(1) average time; page_table_lookup(), page_table_insert(), and page_table_remove() are correctly implemented
BufferPoolStats tracks total fetches, hits, misses, evictions, and dirty writes as uint64_t counters
Hit rate = hits / fetches * 100 is accessible and reports 100% for a workload that re-reads the same page twice with a 2-frame pool

Test suite verifies: (1) cache hit returns same frame pointer as first fetch, (2) LRU evicts page 1 when pages 0,1,2 are loaded then page 0 is re-accessed before page 3 is loaded into a 3-frame pool, (3) pinned pages survive eviction pressure (fetch returns NULL when all frames are pinned), (4) dirty page contents are persisted to disk on eviction and verified by subsequent pread()

Page header occupies exactly 12 bytes with fields: page_type (1 byte), first_freeblock offset (2 bytes big-endian), cell count (2 bytes big-endian), cell content area start (2 bytes big-endian), fragmented bytes (1 byte), and right-child page number (4 bytes big-endian, internal pages only)
Four page types are correctly identified by the page_type byte: TABLE_LEAF=0x0D, TABLE_INTERNAL=0x05, INDEX_LEAF=0x0A, INDEX_INTERNAL=0x02
All multi-byte integers in page headers and cell pointer arrays are encoded big-endian (most-significant byte first), verified by hexdump inspection of a written database file
Cell pointer array begins immediately after the 12-byte page header (at byte 100 on page 0, byte 12 on all other pages) and grows forward with 2-byte big-endian offsets per cell
Cell content area starts at the bottom of the page (offset PAGE_SIZE) and grows backward; new cells are placed at cell_content_area - cell_len and cell_content_area is updated accordingly
page_free_space() correctly computes free bytes as: cell_content_area - (PAGE_HEADER_SIZE + cell_count * 2)
Varint encoder correctly encodes values 0-127 in 1 byte, 128-16383 in 2 bytes, with continuation bit (0x80) set on non-final bytes, and handles the full 64-bit range in at most 9 bytes
Varint decoder correctly recovers all values including negative integers via sign extension for serial types 1-6; round-trip encode/decode is identity for all values 0 through UINT64_MAX
Serial type encoding: NULL=0, integer 0=8, integer 1=9, 1-byte signed integer=1, 2-byte=2, 3-byte=3, 4-byte=4, 6-byte=5, 8-byte=6, IEEE 754 double=7, TEXT=(len*2+13), BLOB=(len*2+12)
Record encoder produces payload with header (header_size varint + serial_type varints) followed by column data; header_size varint value equals total header byte count including itself
Record decoder correctly extracts any column by index by skipping serial types and accumulating data offsets; returns VAL_NULL for column indices beyond the record's column count
TEXT and BLOB values decoded from records point directly into the page buffer (zero-copy); callers must hold the buffer pool pin until they are done using these values
TABLE_LEAF cell format: [payload_size: varint][rowid: varint][payload: record format]
TABLE_INTERNAL cell format: [left_child_page: uint32_t big-endian][separator_rowid: varint]
INDEX_LEAF cell format: [payload_size: varint][payload: record with (key_value, rowid) columns]
INDEX_INTERNAL cell format: [left_child_page: uint32_t big-endian][payload_size: varint][payload: separator key record]

page_insert_cell correctly shifts the cell pointer array to insert at the given position, writes cell bytes into the cell content area, updates cell_count and cell_content_area in the header, and returns -1 when free space is insufficient (including the 2-byte pointer entry)
Table B-tree search (btree_search) descends from root to leaf following separator keys, performs binary search within leaf pages for target rowid, and returns INVALID_PAGE_ID when not found

Leaf split allocates a new page, collects all cells plus the new cell in rowid order, distributes first half to left page and second half to right page, and sets separator_key to the smallest rowid in the right page

After a leaf split, the separator key and right-sibling pointer are correctly inserted into the parent internal page; the parent's right_child pointer is updated when the new separator is the largest key

Root split: when the root page is full and must split, a new root page is allocated with page type TABLE_INTERNAL, containing the separator key and two child pointers (old root as left child, new sibling as right child); the database updates the root_page_id accordingly
btree_cursor_rewind descends to the leftmost leaf by repeatedly following the first cell's left_child pointer; returns 1 (empty) if the root is an empty leaf, 0 otherwise
btree_cursor_next advances to the next cell within the current leaf, or finds the next leaf when the current leaf is exhausted; returns 1 if a next row exists, 0 when all rows are consumed
btree_cursor_column extracts the value at col_idx from the current cell using record_decode_column, keeping the page pinned for the duration the value is in use
Full table scan via cursor interface returns all rows in ascending rowid order; a scan of 1000 inserted rows returns exactly 1000 rows with correct values in the original insertion order
Page 0 begins with the 16-byte SQLite magic string 'SQLite format 3\000' followed by a 2-byte big-endian page size field; the sqlite_master B-tree page header begins at byte 100
Page 1 (sqlite_master root page) is initialized as a TABLE_LEAF page when a new database is created; it uses the same page format as user table pages

schema_register_table inserts a row into sqlite_master with type='table', the table name, tbl_name=name, the root page ID as an integer, and the original SQL string; the row is retrievable by scanning page 1
schema_find_table scans sqlite_master (page 1) and returns the rootpage value for a matching table name, performing case-insensitive comparison; returns INVALID_PAGE_ID if the table does not exist
CREATE TABLE allocates a new page with page_init(PAGE_TYPE_TABLE_LEAF), stores the root page ID in sqlite_master, and stores column definitions for later column-index resolution
After database close and reopen, schema_find_table correctly returns root page IDs for all previously created tables by reading them from the persisted sqlite_master on disk

A database file created by this implementation can be opened by the real sqlite3 command-line tool and SELECT * FROM sqlite_master returns the registered table entries (for simple schemas without overflow)
SELECT * FROM table returns all rows in rowid order via B-tree leaf traversal; row order must match ascending rowid sequence
SELECT col1, col2 deserializes only the requested columns from variable-length records using serial type array scanning; unrequested columns are never decoded
WHERE clause evaluates boolean expressions on deserialized column values using three-valued logic: TRUE includes the row, FALSE and NULL both exclude it
NULL = anything evaluates to NULL (excluded from WHERE results), not FALSE and not TRUE; verified by SELECT returning zero rows for WHERE col = NULL on a table with NULL values
NULL IS NULL evaluates to TRUE; IS NOT NULL evaluates to FALSE for NULL values; these are the only operators that handle NULL non-propagatingly
NOT NULL evaluates to NULL (not TRUE); WHERE NOT (col = 1) does not return rows where col IS NULL
AND truth table correct: NULL AND TRUE = NULL, NULL AND FALSE = FALSE, TRUE AND TRUE = TRUE
OR truth table correct: NULL OR TRUE = TRUE, NULL OR FALSE = NULL, FALSE OR FALSE = FALSE
INSERT adds a row to the B-tree; subsequent SELECT returns the inserted data with all column values matching
INSERT without explicit rowid auto-assigns rowid as MAX(existing_rowid) + 1; rowids are unique and monotonically increasing across sequential inserts
INSERT with NULL for INTEGER PRIMARY KEY triggers auto-increment rowid assignment (not an error)
NOT NULL constraint rejects INSERT that sets a NOT NULL column to NULL; error message names the specific column as 'NOT NULL constraint failed: table.column'
NOT NULL constraint rejects UPDATE that sets a NOT NULL column to NULL with the same error format
UPDATE modifies only the columns named in the SET clause; unmodified columns retain original values
UPDATE with WHERE clause uses three-valued logic: rows where WHERE expression evaluates to NULL are NOT updated
UPDATE cannot change the INTEGER PRIMARY KEY / rowid column; attempt returns an error
UPDATE uses two-pass approach: pass 1 collects matching rowids and deep-copies TEXT/BLOB values, pass 2 applies modifications; this prevents cursor invalidation
DELETE removes rows matching WHERE clause; subsequent SELECT does not return deleted rows
DELETE with WHERE clause uses three-valued logic: rows where WHERE expression is NULL are NOT deleted
DELETE uses two-pass approach: pass 1 collects rowids of matching rows while scan cursor is open, pass 2 calls btree_delete_by_rowid after scan cursor is closed; no cursor corruption
SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on a table not in sqlite_master returns an error containing the table name (e.g., "Table 'nonexistent' does not exist")
TEXT and BLOB column values returned by btree_cursor_column are zero-copy pointers into pinned page memory; the page remains pinned until the cursor advances or closes
Text values used after cursor advancement are deep-copied to heap memory before the cursor advances; no dangling pointer reads
Column projection correctly maps SELECT column names to schema column indices; INSERT with explicit column list (INSERT INTO t (b, a) VALUES ...) maps values to correct column positions
B-tree structure integrity maintained after DELETE: remaining rows are still returned in rowid order by subsequent full table scan
Page defragmentation triggered when a page has insufficient contiguous free space but sufficient total free space (fragmented + contiguous) for a new cell
CREATE INDEX builds a B+tree with leaf nodes storing (indexed_column_value, rowid) pairs and internal nodes storing only separator keys and child pointers (no row data)
CREATE INDEX on a table with existing data correctly populates the index by scanning all existing rows and inserting their (col_value, rowid) pairs in sorted order
Index root page number and original CREATE INDEX SQL are persisted in sqlite_master so the index survives database close and reopen
INSERT into a table automatically inserts a corresponding entry into every index on that table, synchronously within the same operation
DELETE from a table automatically removes the corresponding entry from every index on that table, synchronously and before the table row is removed

UPDATE on an indexed column removes the old (old_value, rowid) index entry and inserts a new (new_value, rowid) index entry; updates that don't touch indexed columns skip index maintenance for those indexes
Index equality lookup (WHERE indexed_col = value) uses OP_INDEX_SEEK to descend the index B+tree and OP_SEEK_ROWID to fetch the full row from the table B+tree (double-lookup pattern)

Index equality lookup reads significantly fewer pages than a full table scan (verified by comparing buffer pool miss counts; index scan must use at least 5x fewer page reads for selective queries on 1000+ row tables)
Index range scan (WHERE indexed_col >= lo AND indexed_col <= hi) uses OP_INDEX_SEEK_GE to find the lower bound then traverses leaf pages via right-sibling pointers until the upper bound is exceeded
Leaf pages in the index B+tree store a right-sibling pointer in the page header, enabling O(1) leaf-to-leaf traversal during range scans without tree re-ascent
UNIQUE index rejects INSERT of a non-null value that already exists in the index with error message 'UNIQUE constraint failed: table.column'
UNIQUE index permits multiple NULL values (NULL != NULL in three-valued logic), so INSERT of NULL into a UNIQUE column always succeeds regardless of existing NULL entries
UNIQUE constraint is checked before the row is inserted into the table B-tree, so UNIQUE violations require no rollback of a partial table insert

The VDBE compiles WHERE predicate on an indexed column into index scan bytecode (OP_OPEN_INDEX, OP_INDEX_SEEK, OP_INDEX_ROWID, OP_SEEK_ROWID) rather than table scan bytecode when a suitable index exists

Covering index detection: when all columns referenced in SELECT and WHERE are present in the index, the compiler omits OP_OPEN_TABLE and OP_SEEK_ROWID, reading all data directly from index leaf entries
index_maintain_delete reads the row's current column values before removing the table row, using those values to locate and remove the correct index entries
For non-unique indexes with duplicate key values, DELETE removes only the (key, rowid) entry matching the specific rowid being deleted, leaving other entries with the same key intact
OP_SEEK_ROWID handles index-table inconsistency gracefully (rowid from index not found in table) by jumping to a not-found address rather than crashing
ANALYZE command performs a full scan of each user table and correctly counts total rows, storing results in sqlite_stat1 with idx=NULL for table-level entries
ANALYZE command scans each index B+tree with a single linear pass and counts the number of distinct key values by detecting value transitions, storing results in sqlite_stat1 with idx=<index_name>
sqlite_stat1 table persists to disk and survives database close/reopen; schema_load reads statistics into an in-memory StatCache on database open
stat_table_rows() returns the analyzed row count for a given table in O(1) from the in-memory cache; returns -1 when no statistics are present
stat_index_distinct() returns the analyzed distinct value count for a given index in O(1) from the in-memory cache; returns -1 when no statistics are present
cost_table_scan(table_name) returns ceil(total_rows / DEFAULT_ROWS_PER_PAGE) estimated sequential page reads; uses DEFAULT_TABLE_ROWS=1000000 when statistics are absent
cost_index_scan(table_name, index_name, selectivity) returns the sum of: B+tree descent depth + leaf pages scanned + matching_rows × RANDOM_IO_COST_FACTOR / rows_per_page
estimate_selectivity() returns 1.0/distinct_values for TOKEN_EQ predicates, approximately 0.33 for TOKEN_LT/TOKEN_GT/TOKEN_LTE/TOKEN_GTE predicates, and DEFAULT_SELECTIVITY when statistics are absent
planner_choose_access_path() evaluates all indexes applicable to the WHERE clause and returns the AccessPath with minimum estimated cost among table scan and all applicable index scans
Planner selects ACCESS_INDEX_SCAN when estimated selectivity is below SELECTIVITY_THRESHOLD (0.20) and an applicable index exists; returns estimated_rows and selectivity in the AccessPath
Planner returns ACCESS_TABLE_SCAN when no index covers the WHERE column, when selectivity exceeds the threshold, or when index scan cost exceeds table scan cost
Planner returns ACCESS_TABLE_SCAN for a WHERE predicate on a column with 2 distinct values (e.g. gender) in a table with 10,000+ rows after ANALYZE
Planner returns ACCESS_INDEX_SCAN for a WHERE equality predicate on a UNIQUE indexed column (1 distinct value per entry) in a table with 10,000+ rows after ANALYZE
Planner falls back to ACCESS_TABLE_SCAN for any WHERE predicate on a non-indexed column, regardless of statistics

EXPLAIN prepended to any valid SQL statement outputs: (1) a plan summary section showing access type, index name if used, selectivity, estimated rows, and estimated cost in pages; (2) the full bytecode dump with addr/opcode/p1/p2/p3 columns
EXPLAIN output explicitly labels plans as 'TABLE SCAN' or 'INDEX SCAN <index_name>' with numeric cost estimates

For multi-table JOIN queries (2+ tables), planner_join_order_dp() uses dynamic programming over bitmask subsets to enumerate all possible join orderings and selects the ordering with minimum total estimated cost
DP join ordering correctly enumerates all non-empty proper subsets of the table set; each partition (left, right) appears exactly once in the enumeration (deduplication via right >= left guard)
Join cardinality estimation uses rows_R × rows_S × (1 / max(distinct_R, distinct_S)) for equi-join conditions

Cardinality estimation errors compound correctly: a 2-table join with 2× base error produces approximately 4× final error; tests verify that larger tables with known data distributions produce planner estimates within 10× of true cardinality
When ANALYZE has not been run, planner uses DEFAULT_TABLE_ROWS=1000000 and DEFAULT_SELECTIVITY=0.01 as fallback; no division by zero occurs on empty tables (distinct_vals=0 guard)
ANALYZE on an empty table (0 rows, 0 distinct values) completes without error and stores '0' as the stat value; subsequent queries on the empty table use table scan with 0 estimated rows
statistics cache invalidates and reloads correctly after ANALYZE completes; queries executed after a second ANALYZE call reflect the updated statistics, not the previous run

The planner/compiler interface is cleanly separated: compile_select accepts an AccessPath struct and emits either table-scan bytecode or index-double-lookup bytecode; the compiler makes no independent access-path decisions
BEGIN transitions transaction state from TXN_STATE_NONE to TXN_STATE_ACTIVE and acquires an exclusive file lock via flock(LOCK_EX)
BEGIN records the current database page count (from file size / PAGE_SIZE) for use in rollback
BEGIN fails with a clear error message when a transaction is already active (no nested transactions)
A .db-journal file named <dbfile>-journal is created lazily on the first write operation within an active transaction, not at BEGIN time
The journal file header contains the 8-byte magic number 0xd9d505f920a163d7, a random nonce, the page count at transaction start, and the page size — all in big-endian byte order
txn_journal_page records the original page contents BEFORE any modification to that page, writing page number (4 bytes) + page data (PAGE_SIZE bytes) + checksum (4 bytes) to the journal
Each page is journaled at most once per transaction — subsequent modifications of an already-journaled page within the same transaction do not add additional journal entries
The checksum in each journal entry covers the page number and page data using the transaction's nonce as the initial value, matching SQLite's checksum algorithm
fsync(journal_fd) is called inside txn_journal_page before the function returns, guaranteeing the journal entry is durable before any database page modification proceeds
COMMIT flushes all dirty pages to the database file via buffer_pool_flush_all(), then calls fsync(db->fd), then deletes the journal via unlink() — in this exact order with no reordering permitted
COMMIT transitions state to TXN_STATE_NONE and releases the file lock via flock(LOCK_UN)
ROLLBACK reads each journal entry, verifies its checksum, and calls pwrite() to restore the original page content to the database file
ROLLBACK calls fsync(db->fd) after restoring all pages to make the rollback durable

ROLLBACK calls buffer_pool_invalidate_dirty_pages() to evict all dirty frames from the buffer pool without writing them to disk, preventing stale rolled-back data from being served to subsequent reads
ROLLBACK deletes the journal file, transitions state to TXN_STATE_NONE, and releases the file lock
Crash recovery in txn_recover() detects a hot journal by checking for the existence of <dbfile>-journal at database open time, before the buffer pool is created
Crash recovery validates the journal header's magic number and rejects journals with invalid magic numbers without modifying the database
Crash recovery restores original pages from valid journal entries (verifying checksums, stopping at the first invalid checksum), calls fsync(db->fd), then deletes the journal
After crash recovery completes, the database contains exactly the state it was in before the crashed transaction began
Auto-commit mode wraps each DML statement in an implicit BEGIN/COMMIT when no explicit transaction is active, ensuring every write is durable before the statement returns

Changes written within an active transaction are not visible to other OS-level processes that open the same database file, because modifications exist only in the buffer pool's dirty frames until COMMIT flushes them to disk

A test suite verifies: (1) ROLLBACK restores exact pre-transaction page content, (2) journal file exists during active transaction and is deleted on COMMIT, (3) crash simulation followed by reopen produces the pre-transaction state, (4) the write ordering invariant is enforced (journal entry + fsync before database write)
Journal entries for pages that were never written to the database file (transaction crashed before flush) are silently skipped during recovery — the database pages are already in their original state
pwrite() is used for all journal and database file writes (not lseek + write) to ensure thread-safe positional I/O
WAL mode appends modified pages as frames to a separate .db-wal file instead of modifying the main database file during write transactions

WAL frame format includes a 24-byte frame header containing page number, database size (non-zero on commit frames), salt pair matching WAL header, and cumulative checksum pair, followed by PAGE_SIZE bytes of page data
WAL header is 32 bytes containing magic number 0x377f0682, file format version, page size, checkpoint sequence number, salt pair, and header checksum
Cumulative two-accumulator checksum is computed across all preceding frames, so any corruption or truncation causes all subsequent checksums to fail
WAL index (in-process hash table) maps page numbers to most recent frame index, enabling O(1) page lookup during read operations
WAL read path checks the WAL index before reading from the main database file, falling back to main database when the page is not found in the WAL
Readers obtain a snapshot marker (end_frame integer) at wal_begin_read() equal to the current WAL frame count at that moment
Readers only see WAL frames with index strictly less than their snapshot marker, implementing snapshot isolation without any reader-writer locking
A reader that begins before a write transaction commits does not see that transaction's changes, even after the commit completes
Multiple readers can execute queries concurrently while a single writer appends frames to the WAL, with no locking between readers and the writer
The writer holds an exclusive WAL write lock preventing concurrent writers from interleaving frames and corrupting the checksum chain
COMMIT in WAL mode calls fsync on the WAL file (not the main database file) making the transaction durable with a single fsync
Buffer pool page loader calls wal_find_frame() before pread() on the main database file when WAL mode is active
Checkpoint (wal_checkpoint / PRAGMA wal_checkpoint) copies WAL frames in order to the main database file using pwrite at the correct page offset
Checkpoint validates frame salts before copying, skipping frames from previous WAL generations
Checkpoint fsyncs the main database file after all frames are copied, making checkpointed pages durable
Checkpoint resets the WAL by writing new random salts to the WAL header, invalidating all existing frames for subsequent readers and writers
Checkpoint resets the WAL index frame_count to 0 after completing, allowing new write transactions to reuse WAL file space
Auto-checkpoint triggers automatically after the WAL reaches a configurable frame count threshold (default 1000 frames)
Auto-checkpoint runs at the end of the write transaction that causes frame_count to exceed the threshold
Checkpoint respects the minimum active reader snapshot and does not copy or invalidate frames that active readers may still need
WAL recovery on database open reads all frames from the WAL file, validating checksums and salts, stopping at the first invalid frame
WAL recovery rebuilds the WAL index from all valid committed frames (frames preceded by a commit frame with non-zero db_size)
Incomplete (uncommitted) frames at the end of the WAL are silently ignored during recovery without error
PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL switches an open database from rollback journal mode to WAL mode, flushing and fsyncing the database first
WAL mode flag is persisted in the database file header so subsequent opens use the correct mode automatically
Corrupted WAL frames (invalid checksum) are detected and rejected; recovery stops at the first corrupt frame rather than using potentially inconsistent data
Frames from previous WAL generations (mismatched salt fields) are detected and ignored during recovery and normal reads
WAL and rollback journal modes are mutually exclusive; switching between them requires no active transactions
The .db-wal file is created when WAL mode is first activated and the WAL receives its first write

criteria.description: COUNT(*) returns the total number of rows in a table, including rows with NULL values in any column
criteria.test_hint: INSERT three rows including one with a NULL column; SELECT COUNT(*) must return 3

criteria.description: COUNT(col) returns the number of non-NULL values in the specified column
criteria.test_hint: Table with 4 rows, 2 having NULL in col; COUNT(col) must return 2

criteria.description: SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX all ignore NULL values; SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX over an all-NULL column returns NULL
criteria.test_hint: Column with values (1, NULL, 3): SUM=4, AVG=2.0, MIN=1, MAX=3. Column all-NULL: SUM=NULL

criteria.description: AVG always returns a REAL (floating-point) value, even when all inputs are INTEGER
COUNT(*) on an empty table returns 0; SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX on an empty table return NULL
criteria.test_hint: Empty table: COUNT(*)=0, SUM(col)=NULL, AVG(col)=NULL, MIN(col)=NULL, MAX(col)=NULL

criteria.description: GROUP BY correctly groups rows by the specified column(s), producing one output row per distinct group
criteria.test_hint: Table with 6 rows across 3 departments; GROUP BY dept produces exactly 3 result rows

criteria.description: Multiple aggregate functions can appear in a single SELECT with GROUP BY
criteria.test_hint: SELECT dept, COUNT(*), AVG(salary), MAX(salary) FROM emp GROUP BY dept returns correct values per group

criteria.description: HAVING filters groups after aggregation; rows excluded by HAVING do not appear in output
criteria.test_hint: HAVING COUNT(*) > 2 on a table with groups of size 1, 2, 3 returns only the group of size 3

criteria.description: WHERE clause filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation — both can appear in one query
criteria.test_hint: SELECT dept, COUNT(*) FROM emp WHERE salary > 50000 GROUP BY dept HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 produces correct results

criteria.description: INNER JOIN with ON condition returns only rows where the join predicate is satisfied
criteria.test_hint: orders(3 rows) JOIN customers(3 rows) ON cust_id=id with one unmatched order returns only matched rows

criteria.description: JOIN with WHERE clause applies additional filtering on top of the join result
criteria.test_hint: JOIN result of 4 rows filtered by WHERE col > value produces correct subset

criteria.description: The VDBE emits OP_OPEN_HASH_AGG, OP_HASH_AGG_STEP, and OP_HASH_AGG_NEXT opcodes for GROUP BY queries
criteria.test_hint: Inspect generated bytecode for a GROUP BY query; confirm hash aggregation opcodes are present

criteria.description: Ungrouped aggregates (no GROUP BY) emit OP_INIT_AGG, OP_AGG_STEP, OP_AGG_FINAL and always return exactly one row
criteria.test_hint: SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(x) FROM t with 0 rows returns one row (0, NULL); with N rows returns one row with correct values

criteria.description: Nested loop JOIN uses two independent cursors; the inner cursor is rewound for each outer row
criteria.test_hint: Trace cursor operations: inner table with M rows and outer with N rows produces M×N cursor steps in the worst case
